title: Training Stability Compared
concept: GANs are notoriously unstable to train (callback to Units 205-208); VAEs and Diffusion models train far more stably with simple, direct loss functions.

exercises.prompt: Remember GANs? What made them so tricky to train?

options.text: Two networks (generator vs discriminator) fighting each other, which can go unstable
They only had one network so there was nothing to balance
They didn't use any loss function at all

exercises.explain: Right! GANs pit a generator against a discriminator in a constant tug-of-war, which makes training a delicate balancing act.

cards.title: Some Models Train Like a Rollercoaster
cards.body: Back in Units 205-208, you learned GANs are powerful but wild to train — like riding a rollercoaster that might jump the tracks. Today: meet the calmer cousins, VAEs and Diffusion models, that train more like a smooth train ride.

cards.title: Training Stability
cards.body: Training stability means how smoothly and reliably a model's loss goes down during training. GANs have two competing networks that can spiral out of balance. VAEs and Diffusion models use one simple, direct loss goal, so they usually improve steadily without blowing up.

cards.title: Tug-of-War vs. Solo Practice
cards.body: Training a GAN is like a tug-of-war between two teams — if one team gets too strong too fast, the rope snaps and everything falls apart. Training a VAE or Diffusion model is like a solo athlete practicing free throws — just you, a clear goal, and steady improvement shot after shot.

cards.title: Watching the Loss Curves
cards.body: If you plotted loss over time: a GAN's generator and discriminator losses often bounce up and down unpredictably, sometimes never settling. A VAE or Diffusion model's loss usually slides down in a smooth curve, epoch after epoch, like a ball rolling gently downhill.

cards.title: Myth: Unstable Training Means GANs Are Useless
cards.body: Not true! GANs can produce amazing, sharp images when trained well — but it takes careful tuning, tricks, and patience. VAEs and Diffusion aren't 'better' at everything; they're just easier and more predictable to train, which is a huge practical advantage.

cards.title: Two Training Styles Side by Side
cards.body: Compare how each model type behaves while learning:

left.text: Two networks compete; loss can swing wildly, sometimes collapses or never converges

right.text: One clear loss to minimize; loss curve is smooth and steadily improves

exercises.prompt: Why do VAEs and Diffusion models tend to train more stably than GANs?

options.text: They use a simple, direct loss function instead of two competing networks
They don't use any loss function
They train on smaller datasets only
They avoid using neural networks entirely

exercises.explain: VAEs and Diffusion models optimize one clear objective, so there's no adversarial tug-of-war causing instability like in GANs.

exercises.prompt: GANs are generally considered easier and more stable to train than VAEs or Diffusion models.
exercises.explain: False — it's the opposite! GANs are known for unstable training due to their two competing networks, while VAEs and Diffusion train more smoothly.

exercises.sentence: GANs can be unstable because two networks ___ against each other, while VAEs and Diffusion models use a single steady loss.
exercises.explain: In a GAN, the generator and discriminator compete, which can cause training to become unstable.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best captures the key difference in training stability?

options.text: VAEs/Diffusion use one direct loss and train smoothly; GANs use two competing networks and can be unstable
GANs and VAEs both use identical loss functions so they train the same way
Diffusion models don't use loss functions, so they can't be compared to GANs

exercises.explain: The core difference is the adversarial setup in GANs versus the single, direct objective in VAEs and Diffusion models.

exercises.prompt: A student trains a GAN and notices the loss values jumping around wildly, never settling down. They then train a Diffusion model on the same data and see a smooth, steadily decreasing loss curve. What's the best explanation?

options.text: The GAN's two competing networks (generator vs discriminator) create an unstable balancing act, while the Diffusion model optimizes one simple, direct loss
The Diffusion model is smaller so it always trains faster
GANs always fail and never produce useful results
The student made an error; both models should have identical loss curves

exercises.explain: This is the key takeaway: GANs' adversarial two-network setup often causes unstable, jumpy training, while VAEs and Diffusion models' single clear loss objective leads to smoother, more predictable training.
